title: Nearest Neighbor Search
concept: Once images are represented as embeddings, finding similar images becomes a nearest neighbor search problem: locating the database vectors closest to the query vector in embedding space, typically using distance measures like cosine similarity.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why do embedding vectors give a better similarity signal than raw pixel comparison?

options.text: Embeddings capture high-level visual meaning robust to angle, lighting, and minor cropping changes
Embeddings are always larger files than raw images
Embeddings ignore all image content entirely

exercises.explain: Yes! Embeddings robustly encode visual meaning, unlike raw pixels.

cards.title: Finding your closest neighbors on a map
cards.body: Once every image in your database is just a point (embedding vector) in a big multi-dimensional 'meaning space', finding similar images becomes a very concrete geometry problem: which points are closest to my query point?

cards.title: Nearest Neighbor Search
cards.body: Nearest neighbor search finds the database embedding vectors that are closest to a query embedding vector, using a distance measure such as cosine similarity (angle between vectors) or Euclidean distance (straight-line distance).

cards.title: Finding the closest coffee shops on a map
cards.body: If your embedding vector is a pin dropped on a map, nearest neighbor search is exactly like asking a map app 'show me the 5 closest coffee shops to this pin.' Distance on the map (in this case, in embedding space) directly translates into visual similarity.

cards.title: Top-5 similar handbag search
cards.body: A user uploads a photo of a handbag. The system computes its embedding, then finds the 5 closest embeddings in a database of 10 million product photos, returning those 5 handbags as the most visually similar matches.

cards.title: Distance metric choice actually matters
cards.body: It's a myth that any distance measure works equally well. Cosine similarity (which ignores vector magnitude, focusing purely on direction) is often preferred over raw Euclidean distance for embeddings, because it's less sensitive to overall brightness/scale differences between images.

cards.title: Cosine Similarity vs. Euclidean Distance
cards.body: Two common ways to measure closeness:

left.text:
Measures the ANGLE between vectors
Ignores overall magnitude/scale
Common default for embeddings

right.text:
Measures straight-line distance
Sensitive to vector magnitude
Simple, geometric intuition

exercises.prompt: What does nearest neighbor search find in image retrieval?

options.text: The database embedding vectors closest to the query embedding vector
The exact pixel-for-pixel duplicate of the query image only
The largest file in the database
A random sample of unrelated images

exercises.explain: Correct! It finds the closest embedding vectors, representing the most visually similar images.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors rather than their raw magnitude difference.
exercises.explain: True. Cosine similarity focuses on direction, making it less sensitive to overall scale/brightness differences.

exercises.sentence: Nearest neighbor search finds the closest database vectors to a query vector using a ___ measure.
exercises.explain: A distance measure (like cosine similarity or Euclidean distance) quantifies closeness between embedding vectors.

exercises.prompt: Why might cosine similarity be preferred over raw Euclidean distance for comparing image embeddings?

options.text: Cosine similarity ignores overall magnitude/scale, making it less sensitive to brightness or intensity differences
Cosine similarity always returns exactly zero for any two images
Euclidean distance cannot be computed for vectors at all

exercises.explain: Correct! Cosine similarity's magnitude-independence makes it robust to certain nuisance variations.

exercises.prompt: A photo search engine returns visually odd results: very dark photos are matched with other very dark photos regardless of actual content, ignoring subject similarity. What might fix this?

options.text: Switching from a magnitude-sensitive distance metric to cosine similarity, which focuses on content direction rather than overall brightness/intensity
Deleting the entire embedding model and using random matching instead
Increasing the image file size before searching
Nothing can be done; this behavior is unavoidable in image retrieval

exercises.explain: Exactly! Distance metric choice directly affects what 'similar' means to the system. Next: putting all these pieces together into a real reverse image search engine.
